document: Introduction To Internet Of Things JAN 2026

questions.questionText: State whether the following statement is true or false. Statement: There is no network layer in Wired HART [cite: 2794]
questions.explanation: Wired HART uses a simplified three-layer stack (Physical, Data Link, and Application) because it relies on direct point-to-point or multi-drop wiring[cite: 2809]. Since it doesn't need to route data through a complex mesh, a dedicated Network layer is unnecessary[cite: 2810].

questions.questionText: Zwave operates at ....... MHz in the US and .... MHz in Europe. [cite: 2811, 2816]
questions.explanation: Z-Wave uses sub-GHz frequencies to avoid interference with the crowded 2.4 GHz band[cite: 2825]. It is specifically tuned to 908.42 MHz in the US and 868.42 MHz in Europe to comply with different regional radio regulations[cite: 2826].

questions.questionText: Dumb behavior of sensor nodes is ...... in nature (as it is dependent on the effects of the environmental conditions). [cite: 2827, 2828]
questions.explanation: The behavior of sensor nodes is called temporal because it changes over time based on environmental conditions[cite: 2841]. Factors like temperature, humidity, or light vary with time, affecting sensor responses[cite: 2842]. Thus, the behavior depends on time-based changes rather than location[cite: 2842, 2844].

questions.questionText: The process of identifying channels consistently affected by interference and removing them from use is known as... [cite: 2846]
questions.explanation: The process described is generally known as channel blacklisting or channel hopping/avoidance, not "channel trimming" or "channel flushing."[cite: 2858]. These terms are not standard for interference management in networks[cite: 2859].

questions.questionText: The full form of NFC is [cite: 2860]
questions.explanation: NFC stands for Near Field Communication[cite: 2877]. It is a short-range wireless technology used for data exchange between devices[cite: 2877]. It is commonly used in contactless payments and smart cards[cite: 2878].

questions.questionText: State true or false. NFC works on the principle of magnetic induction. [cite: 2879, 2880]
questions.explanation: NFC (Near Field Communication) uses magnetic induction between two loop antennas located within each other's "near field."[cite: 2889]. This allows the initiator to create a magnetic field that powers and communicates with a passive target over a very short distance[cite: 2890].

questions.questionText: State whether the following statement is true or false. Statement: NFC tags found in supermarket products are examples of active NFC. [cite: 2893, 2894]
questions.explanation: Supermarket tags are examples of passive NFC[cite: 2907]. They do not have their own power source and instead rely on the radio frequency field generated by an active reader (like a smartphone or scanner) to transmit their data[cite: 2907].

Bluetooth is actually based on a technology called Piconets[cite: 2922]. A Piconet is a small ad-hoc network formed when one "master" device connects with up to seven "slave" devices using frequency-hopping spread spectrum[cite: 2922].

questions.questionText: A ...... in NFC emits a small electric current which creates a magnetic field that in turn bridges the physical space between the devices. [cite: 2923, 2924, 2925]
questions.explanation: In an NFC connection, the reader (active device) emits an electric current through its internal coil[cite: 2936, 2940]. This generates a magnetic field that induces power in the receiving tag, allowing data to be exchanged wirelessly over very short distances[cite: 2940].

questions.questionText: State true of false. Bluetooth technology is based on Ad-hoc network technology. [cite: 2941, 2942]
questions.explanation: Bluetooth uses ad-hoc technology because it allows devices to form spontaneous, short-range connections (Piconets) without needing a central router or pre-existing infrastructure[cite: 2950]. These networks are created on-the-fly as soon as devices come within range of each other[cite: 2951].

questions.questionText: State true or false. Zwave utilizes GFSK modulation and Hongkong channel encoding. [cite: 2952, 2953]
questions.explanation: While Z-Wave does utilize GFSK (Gaussian Frequency Shift Keying) for modulation, it uses Manchester or NRZ (Non-Return-to-Zero) encoding, not "Hongkong channel encoding."[cite: 2964]. There is no such standard as Hongkong channel encoding in the Z-Wave protocol[cite: 2965].

questions.questionText: The process of bypassing radio dead-spots is done using a message called ... In Zwave. [cite: 2966]
questions.explanation: In a Z-Wave mesh network, "Healing" is the process where the controller re-evaluates the signal paths between nodes[cite: 2978, 2981]. It allows the system to automatically find a new route for data, effectively bypassing radio dead-spots or moved devices to maintain a stable connection[cite: 2981].

questions.questionText: State whether the following statement is true or false. Statement: Sensor nodes have unlimited battery life. [cite: 2982]
questions.explanation: Sensor nodes are typically small, battery-powered devices with a very limited energy supply[cite: 2994]. Managing this power is a major challenge in loT, as the batteries eventually drain and often need to be replaced or recharged to keep the network functional[cite: 2995].

questions.questionText: State whether the following statement is true or false. Statement: The Link Manager Protocol in Bluetooth manages the establishment, authentication, link configuration. [cite: 2996, 2997]
questions.explanation: The Link Manager Protocol (LMP) is responsible for setting up and controlling the radio link between Bluetooth devices[cite: 2999]. It handles the low-level technical details like authentication, pairing, encryption, and the negotiation of packet sizes to ensure a secure and stable connection[cite: 3000].

Zigbee is designed for low-power, low-bandwidth applications[cite: 3009]. Operating on the 2.4 GHz ISM band, it supports a maximum over-the-air data rate of 250 kbps, which is ideal for transmitting simple sensor data or control commands in a smart home or industrial setting[cite: 3009].
